planting.note: Plant divisions in early fall or spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Frances Williams is a slow-growing giant that takes 3–4 years to reach full size but is extremely long-lived and virtually indestructible once established.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in dappled to full shade; Frances Williams is one of the most celebrated and widely grown hostas in the world, a giant with distinctive round, deeply puckered, blue-green leaves edged with an irregular golden-to-chartreuse margin 1–3 inches wide. The thick, waxy leaf texture provides moderate slug resistance, a notable advantage over thinner-leafed cultivars. It is a slow grower that requires 3–4 years to develop its full character — a mound 2–2.5 feet tall and 4–5 feet wide that becomes the focal point of any shaded garden. Pale lavender flowers appear in midsummer on tall scapes above the foliage but are considered secondary to the bold leaf display. In zone 8–9, situate it where morning sun reaches the leaves but where it is protected from harsh afternoon heat, and maintain consistent moisture to prevent leaf scorch on the margins. Mulch heavily with wood chips to retain moisture and suppress competing weeds.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to dappled shade; Royal Standard is one of the most heat-tolerant hostas and performs better in zone 8–9 than most cultivars, particularly when given consistent moisture and morning sun with afternoon shade.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Royal Standard is an exceptional hosta combining fragrance, heat tolerance, and adaptability in a single reliable cultivar — it produces large, glossy, bright-green, heart-shaped leaves and, in late summer, pure white, intensely fragrant funnel-shaped flowers that perfume the evening garden. It is a hybrid of H. plantaginea that inherits the species' notable heat and sun tolerance, making it one of the best hostas for zone 7–9 gardens where many blue-leafed cultivars decline in summer heat. Royal Standard will tolerate more sun than most hostas as long as soil moisture is maintained, even growing in quite open shade near the edge of the woodland garden. The outstanding fragrance of the late-summer flowers makes it an invaluable plant near sitting areas, patios, and paths where the evening scent can be enjoyed. Divide every 4–5 years when the central crown begins to decline.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Krossa Regal develops a distinctive upright vase-shaped form rather than the mounded habit of most hostas, creating an architectural accent in the shade garden.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Krossa Regal is among the most structurally distinct hostas — rather than forming the typical low mound, it produces large, strongly upright, arching, glaucous blue-gray leaves that create a dramatic vase-shaped form 28–36 inches tall and up to 5 feet wide at maturity. This architectural habit makes it an outstanding focal point in shaded borders, particularly when contrasted with the mounding form of surrounding plants. The tall flower scapes — often reaching 4–5 feet — produce pale lavender flowers that are among the most ornamental of any hosta. The thick, waxy, blue-gray leaf surface is notably resistant to slug damage. It is slow to establish but very long-lived, forming impressive, multi-crowned clumps over many years. Provide consistent moisture and mulch heavily; the upright leaves funnel rainwater to the crown, helping maintain soil moisture during dry spells.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Big Daddy's heavy, deeply puckered leaves are among the most slug-resistant of any large hosta, making it excellent for gardens where slug pressure is high.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Big Daddy is a prized large hosta selected from H. sieboldiana known for exceptionally large, deeply cupped and heavily puckered, powder-blue leaves that hold their blue color well into summer. The thick, waxy leaf texture is among the most slug-resistant available in a large hosta, making it the preferred choice for gardeners who struggle with slug damage. The cupped leaf surface collects dew and light rain, giving the crown water directly. Pale lavender to nearly white flowers appear in midsummer and are attractive though secondary to the bold foliage. Big Daddy is a steady grower that requires 3 years to reach its best appearance but is extremely long-lived. Mulch with 2–3 inches of wood chips to keep roots cool and moist; consistent moisture is essential for maintaining leaf quality and preventing edge browning in warm weather.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full to partial shade; Blue Angel is a giant that requires generous spacing — allow at least 5 feet in each direction — and takes 4–5 years to achieve its spectacular full size.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in deep to partial shade; Blue Angel is a giant hosta of the first rank — at maturity forming a massive mound of enormous, deeply veined, glaucous powder-blue to blue-gray leaves that can measure 18 inches long by 14 inches wide. The overall clump reaches 3–4 feet tall and 5–6 feet wide in ideal conditions, making it one of the largest blue hostas in cultivation. The extremely thick, waxy leaf cuticle provides exceptional slug resistance and helps maintain the characteristic blue color through summer, as the waxy bloom reflects blue wavelengths while absorbing others. Near-white, lightly fragrant flowers appear in midsummer on tall, attractive scapes. In zone 8–9, consistent moisture and deep shade are essential to maintain leaf quality through summer; a large, deep mulch layer helps keep roots cool. A single established clump is a spectacular focal point in any shade garden.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic, very humus-rich, well-drained soil in full to partial shade; Empress Wu is the largest known hosta and requires enormous spacing — allow 6–7 feet in each direction — taking 5+ years to reach full impressive size.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, very humus-rich, well-drained soil in deep to partial shade; Empress Wu holds the distinction of being the largest named hosta cultivar in existence, with individual leaves measuring up to 18 inches long by 15 inches wide on petioles that themselves can reach 24 inches — the overall plant forms a magnificent mound 3–4 feet tall and 5–7 feet wide after 5+ years of establishment. The large, dark olive-green, heavily textured, deeply veined leaves are outstanding. Pale lavender flowers are produced on tall scapes in midsummer. This hosta is a commitment — it requires years of patience before reaching its full potential — but the result is arguably the most impressive deciduous perennial a shade garden can display. Consistent moisture, deep organic mulch, and protection from afternoon heat in zone 7–8 are essential; in zone 9 it is marginal and benefits from cooling in the hottest months. Empress Wu does not divide easily for several years after planting.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Guacamole tolerates more sun than most hostas and is notably fragrant, making it an excellent choice for the shadier edges of sunny borders and near seating areas where the evening fragrance can be enjoyed.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Guacamole is a medium-large hosta sporting distinctive avocado-green to chartreuse leaf centers with darker, forest-green margins — the reverse color pattern of most variegated hostas. It was selected as the American Hosta Growers Association Hosta of the Year in 2002. Most notably, it inherits outstanding fragrance from its H. 'Fragrant Bouquet' ancestry — near-white, intensely sweet-scented flowers on tall scapes in late summer that are exceptional near sitting areas. Guacamole also tolerates more sun than most hostas, performing well in dappled to partial shade at the sunny edge of a woodland garden as long as soil moisture is consistent. The center of the leaf gradually intensifies to a bright gold-green through summer where it receives light, creating a striking seasonal color change. A fast-growing, vigorous cultivar that establishes quickly compared to larger, slower hostas.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Liberty is a large, show-stopping hosta that requires 3–4 years to develop full size and leaf character — the wide gold margin becomes more prominent and the overall plant more impressive with each passing season.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Liberty is a large hosta with a striking bicolor pattern — blue-green, deeply corrugated, puckered centers surrounded by an exceptionally wide, 3–4 inch irregular margin that transitions from creamy white in spring through gold and yellow in summer. It was named the American Hosta Growers Association Hosta of the Year in 2012. The very wide margin distinguishes it from Frances Williams (its likely parent) and creates a bolder, more dramatic variegation. Pale lavender flowers appear on tall scapes in midsummer. Like all large hostas with H. sieboldiana background, Liberty performs best when given generous space, consistent moisture, and deep organic mulch. The wide margin may show some margin burn in zone 8–9 during prolonged summer heat, making deep shade and consistent moisture essential in warm climates. A spectacular long-lived perennial for mature shade gardens.
